sanskrit_iast:
rāga-dveṣa-viyuktais tu viṣayān indriyaiś caran |
ātma-vaśyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigacchati || 64 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.64।।)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'ragadvesa-viyuktais tu visayan indriyais caran atmavasyair vidheyatma prasadam adhigacchati')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.064 = MBh_06,024.064). Body text identical: 'rāgadveṣaviyuktais tu viṣayān indriyaiś caran / ātmavaśyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigacchati'. GRETIL writes the compound 'rāgadveṣaviyuktais' as one word where vedabase prints 'rāga-dveṣa-viyuktais' with hyphens; same compound.

anvaya.meaning: freed-from / disjoined-from (in compound rāga-dveṣa-viyuktaiḥ: 'with [senses] freed from attachment-and-aversion')
under control (in compound ātma-vaśyaiḥ: 'with [senses] under one's own control')
self (in compound vidheya-ātmā: 'one whose self is disciplined')

translations.verbatim_quote: But a person free from all attachment and aversion and able to control his senses through regulative principles of freedom can obtain the complete mercy of the Lord.
But one who controls the mind, and is free from attachment and aversion, even while using the objects of the senses, attains the Grace of God.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: It is already explained that one may externally control the senses by some artificial process, but unless the senses are engaged in the transcendental service of the Lord, there is every chance of a fall. Although the person in full Kṛṣṇa consciousness may apparently be on the sensual plane, because of his being Kṛṣṇa conscious, he has no attachment to sensual activities.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Shankara bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Certainly the functions of the organs are naturally preceded by attraction and repulsion. This being so, caran, by perceiving; visayan, objects, which are unavoidable; indriyaih, with the organs such as ears etc.; raga-dvesa-viyuktaih, that are free from those attraction and repulsion; and are atma-vasyaih, under his own control; vidheya-atma, [one whose] mind is amenable to control; adhigacchati, attains; prasadam, serenity.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the alternative to the cascade in 2.62-2.63. Sense-objects are unavoidable — the operator cannot escape them — but the operator who moves through them with senses freed from attraction (rāga) and aversion (dveṣa), whose senses are under his own control (ātma-vaśyaiḥ), and whose mind is amenable to discipline (vidheya-ātmā), attains prasāda (serenity, mental clarity). Shankara stresses that the verse describes engaged action, not retreat: the operator perceives the objects with the senses but is not captured by attraction-and-aversion to them.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Ramanuja bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Having focussed, in the way already described, the mind on Me — the Lord of all and the auspicious object of meditation, he who goes through, i.e., considers with contempt the sense-objects, with senses under control and free from hate and attraction by reason of all impurities of mind being burnt out — such a person has a disciplined self, i.e., disciplined mind. He attains serenity. The meaning is that his mind will be free of impurities.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as the consequence of the prescription in 2.61. Once the mind is focussed on the Supreme as the auspicious object, the mind's impurities are burnt out, the senses are no longer pulled by attachment-and-aversion, and the operator can engage with sense-objects without being captured by them. The 'disciplined self' (vidheya-ātmā) is the mind that has been brought under control through the meditative focus. Prasāda (serenity) is the consequence — the mind free of impurities.

positions.rendering: able to control his senses through regulative principles of freedom
controls the mind ... using the objects of the senses
with senses under his own control (ātma-vaśyaiḥ); whose mind is amenable to control (vidheya-ātmā)
such a person has a disciplined self, i.e., disciplined mind (mind purified through focus on the Supreme)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The two ātma-compounds in the verse name distinct things. ātma-vaśyaiḥ qualifies the senses ('senses under one's own control'). vidheya-ātmā qualifies the agent ('one whose self/mind is disciplined'). Shankara preserves both. Modern translations (Prabhupada, Mukundananda) tend to fold the two into one expression ('controls his senses' / 'controls the mind'), which collapses the verse's two-fold structure. The lexical force is: the senses are under control AND the agent is themselves disciplined. Both halves are required.

positions.rendering: the complete mercy of the Lord
serenity (prasāda as the operator's mental state)
serenity (the consequence of mind freed from impurities)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: prasāda is lexically ambiguous between (a) the mental state of the operator (clarity, serenity, placid composure) and (b) the favour or grace of a divine being. Shankara and Ramanuja both render the (a) reading as primary in this verse — serenity is the operator's attained state — while Prabhupada and Mukundananda foreground the (b) reading in their translations. The next verse (2.65) continues the chain with prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṁ hānir ('in serenity, the destruction of all sorrows') which makes the (a) reading lexically tighter for 2.64: the prasāda attained here is what the operator possesses in 2.65, and only the (a) reading lets that work.

literal_meaning: But moving among sense-objects with the senses freed from attachment-and-aversion, with senses under one's own control, the disciplined self attains serenity.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse names the alternative to the cascade in 2.62-2.63. The operator does not retreat from sense-objects (viṣayas are unavoidable, per Shankara) but moves through them — with senses freed from rāga (attachment) and dveṣa (aversion), with senses under one's own control (ātma-vaśyaiḥ), as one whose self is disciplined (vidheya-ātmā). The result is prasāda — serenity, mental clarity. Shankara emphasises this is engaged action, not retreat. Ramanuja reads the disciplined self as the consequence of the meditative focus prescribed in 2.61: once the mind is on the Supreme, the impurities burn out, and the senses can engage objects without being captured. Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds prasāda into 'the mercy of the Lord'. All traditions agree that prasāda is the consequence — the operator's mental state — and that the sense-objects are perceived, not avoided.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable.
